disease.name: Liver Abscess (Pyogenic and Amoebic)
disease.note: Focal suppurative or amoebic collection within the liver parenchyma. Pyogenic abscess: polymicrobial (E. coli, Klebsiella, anaerobes, strep) often from biliary tract or portal vein (diverticulitis, appendicitis). Amoebic abscess: Entamoeba histolytica, typically single right lobe lesion. Both require prompt diagnosis with drainage and antimicrobial therapy.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Septic shock or ruptured -> EMERGENCY ICU + drainage; Large (>5cm) or non-responsive -> URGENT drainage; Small single -> URGENT antibiotics + may drain

historyFeatures.note: Amoebic abscess may occur months after dysentery or without colitis history

subtypes.name: Pyogenic Liver Abscess
subtypes.note: Most common type in Western countries. Etiology: biliary tract (50% via ascending cholangitis), portal vein (20% via diverticulitis, appendicitis, IBD), hepatic artery (20% via bacteremia), direct extension. Common organisms: E. coli, Klebsiella pneumoniae (especially in diabetics), anaerobes (Bacteroides, Clostridium), Streptococcus spp., Enterococcus.

subtypes.name: Amoebic Liver Abscess
subtypes.note: Caused by Entamoeba histolytica. Complication of intestinal amoebiasis (though 50% have no diarrhea history). Typically single right lobe abscess. Contents: sterile 'anchovy paste' (brown-red, non-foul). Risk of rupture into pleura/pericardium/peritoneum. Highly prevalent in India, Africa, Southeast Asia, Central/South America.

bedside.name: Temperature, heart rate, blood pressure, RR, SpO2 monitoring
Blood cultures (aerobic + anaerobic) x2 sets before antibiotics
bedside.indication: identify causative organism and antibiotic sensitivity

laboratory.name: FBC with differential
laboratory.indication: leukocytosis; left shift; anemia of chronic disease

laboratory.name: LFTs (ALT, AST, ALP, GGT, bilirubin)
laboratory.indication: elevated ALP and GGT common (space-occupying lesion); ALT/AST may be mildly elevated; bilirubin elevated if biliary obstruction

laboratory.name: Serum amoebic serology (E. histolytica antibodies)
laboratory.indication: positive in >90% of amoebic liver abscess; negative in pyogenic; may remain positive for years

laboratory.name: Stool microscopy for ova and parasites + E. histolytica antigen/PCR
laboratory.indication: only 50% of amoebic abscess patients have concurrent intestinal infection

laboratory.name: Procalcitonin
laboratory.indication: elevated in bacterial infection; may help distinguish pyogenic from amoebic

imaging.name: Abdominal ultrasound with contrast (if needed)
imaging.indication: first-line: round/oval hypoechoic lesion; internal echoes; enhanced through-transmission; wall may be thick (amoebic) or irregular (pyogenic); layering debris/sediment level

imaging.name: CT abdomen with IV contrast
imaging.indication: characteristic low-attenuation lesion with rim enhancement; 'cluster sign' (small abscesses coalescing into larger cavity); gas bubbles inside suggests pyogenic; extrahepatic extension assessment

imaging.name: MRI abdomen (if CT contraindicated)
imaging.indication: high T2 signal; restricted diffusion; rim enhancement on gadolinium

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.indication: right pleural effusion, elevated right hemidiaphragm, basilar atelectasis

imaging.name: US or CT-guided aspiration/drainage
imaging.indication: diagnostic + therapeutic. Pus for Gram stain, culture (aerobic + anaerobic), sensitivity. Amoebic: 'anchovy paste' appearance, no organisms on Gram stain, sterile culture.

management.protocolReference: AASLD Practice Guidelines: Hepatic Abscess; WHO Guidelines: Amoebiasis

pathways.criteria: Pyogenic abscess <5cm in diameter, no signs of sepsis, no biliary obstruction, single lesion
pathways.treatment: IV antibiotics alone may suffice. Empiric: ceftriaxone 2g IV q24h OR cefotaxime 2g IV q8h + metronidazole 500mg IV q8h (covers anaerobes). Alternative: piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h. Duration: 2-4 weeks IV, then 2-4 weeks oral (guided by clinical response and cultures). Monitor with imaging q1-2 weeks; if enlarging or no response, proceed to drainage. If poor clinical response at 48-72h: add percutaneous drainage.

pathways.criteria: Abscess >5cm, multiple/bilobar abscesses, septic presentation, or failed medical therapy after 48-72h
pathways.treatment: Percutaneous catheter drainage (PCD) under US/CT guidance combined with IV antibiotics. Use pigtail catheter (8-14Fr). Send pus for Gram stain, culture, and sensitivity. Empiric: ceftriaxone 2g IV q24h + metronidazole 500mg IV q8h. Adjust based on culture results. If Klebsiella: ceftriaxone or carbapenem if ESBL. If enterococcus: ampicillin or vancomycin. If drainage inadequate or multiloculated, consider endoscopic transpapillary drainage (if biliary communication) or surgical drainage. Duration: until afebrile 48h + imaging resolution. Typically 4-6 weeks total antibiotics (IV then oral transition based on oral bioavailability).

pathways.criteria: Amoebic serology positive, typical imaging (single right lobe, 'anchovy paste' aspiration), +/- dysentery history
pathways.treatment: First-line: metronidazole 750mg IV/PO q8h (adults) or 35-50mg/kg/day PO divided q8h (children) x 10-14 days. Alternative: tinidazole 2g PO daily x 3-5 days (shorter course, better tolerated). Followed by intraluminal cyst eradication: paromomycin 25-35mg/kg/day PO divided q8h x 7 days OR iodoquinol 650mg PO TID x 20 days OR diloxanide furoate 500mg PO TID x 10 days (to prevent intestinal carrier state and relapse). Percutaneous drainage: reserve for large abscess >10cm, left lobe (risk of pericardial rupture), impending rupture, or lack of clinical response to metronidazole after 72h. Therapeutic aspiration: may be done for large abscesses to speed resolution. Rupture into pleura: add chest tube. Rupture into pericardium: surgical drainage.

pathways.criteria: Peritoneal rupture (acute abdomen), pleural rupture (empyema/dyspnea), pericardial rupture (tamponade)
pathways.treatment: Surgical emergency. Laparotomy + drainage + source control + IV antibiotics. Peritoneal: surgical lavage + drainage. Pleural: chest tube + drainage. Pericardial: pericardial window + drainage. Hemodynamic support and ICU management. Broad-spectrum antibiotics (ceftriaxone + metronidazole or piperacillin-tazobactam).

pathways.criteria: No clinical improvement after 72h of antibiotics + drainage, or abscess non-drainable percutaneously
pathways.treatment: Re-image to assess for undrained loculations or new abscesses. Consider endoscopic transpapillary drainage if biliary communication. Consider surgical drainage (laparoscopic or open) if percutaneous not feasible. Re-culture to identify resistant organisms. Consider fungal abscess (especially in immunocompromised). Prolonged antibiotic course (6-8 weeks). Look for source of seeding (colonoscopy for diverticulosis/IBD, ERCP for biliary source, echocardiography for endocarditis).

disease.emergencyPriority: high if septic or ruptured; medium if complex; medium-high overall for pyogenic
